lessons.title: Types of Lines
lessons.intro: Look around your classroom — the edges of your notebook, the lines on a ruled page, the railway tracks outside. Lines are everywhere! In geometry, we study three special ways two lines can relate to each other.
lessons.visual: Three pairs of lines: parallel (railway tracks), perpendicular (a '+' crossroad), intersecting (scissors blades crossing).

steps.text: Parallel lines run side by side and never meet, no matter how far you extend them.
steps.tip: Think of railway tracks or the opposite edges of your ruler — they stay the same distance apart forever.

steps.text: Perpendicular lines meet at exactly 90° — a perfect right angle.
steps.tip: The corner of your textbook or the T in a cricket stump and the bail forms a perpendicular.

steps.text: Intersecting lines cross each other at any angle. If that angle is 90°, they are also perpendicular.
steps.notation: \text{Lines } l_1 \text{ and } l_2 \text{ intersect at point } P

explanation: Parallel lines have the same direction and never cross. Symbol: ∥.
Perpendicular lines form a 90° angle at their meeting point. Symbol: ⊥.
Intersecting lines share exactly one common point.
lessons.rule: Two lines in a plane are either parallel, perpendicular, or intersecting at some other angle.
lessons.tip: Look at a rangoli pattern: the straight border lines are parallel, and the cross-lines inside are often perpendicular!

lessons.title: What Is an Angle?
lessons.intro: When you open a book, the two covers form an angle. When a batsman lifts the bat, the bat and the ground form an angle. An angle is simply the amount of turn between two rays sharing a common starting point.
lessons.visual: Two rays OA and OB starting from point O, with a curved arc marking the angle between them.

steps.text: An angle is formed by two rays (called arms) meeting at a common point (called the vertex).
steps.notation: \angle AOB \text{ — vertex } O, \text{ arms } OA \text{ and } OB

steps.text: We measure angles in degrees (°). A full turn is 360°.
The angle is named by the vertex letter (middle) with one arm letter on each side, or just by the vertex if there is no confusion.
steps.tip: Always put the vertex letter in the middle when using three letters.

explanation: Two rays with a common endpoint form an angle.
The common endpoint is the vertex; the rays are the arms.
Angles are measured in degrees using a protractor.
lessons.rule: An angle measures the amount of rotation from one arm to another about the vertex.
lessons.tip: Imagine the hands of a clock: at 3 o'clock they form a 90° angle, and at 6 o'clock they form a 180° angle.

lessons.title: Types of Angles
lessons.intro: Not all angles are the same size. A door slightly open makes a small angle; a door flung wide makes a large one. Let's classify angles by their size.
lessons.visual: Five angles drawn in a row: acute (40°), right (90°), obtuse (120°), straight (180°), reflex (300°).

steps.text: An acute angle is less than 90°. Think of the tip of a sharpened pencil.
A right angle is exactly 90°. We mark it with a small square at the vertex.
steps.tip: The corners of your NCERT textbook are all right angles!

steps.text: An obtuse angle is between 90° and 180°. Imagine the hands of a clock at 10:10.
A straight angle is exactly 180° — the arms form a straight line.
A reflex angle is between 180° and 360°. It is the 'bigger' angle when you look at the outside of an ordinary angle.
steps.tip: If someone asks for the reflex angle when the normal angle is 60°, it is 360° − 60° = 300°.

explanation: Acute: 0° to 90° (exclusive).
Right: exactly 90°.
Obtuse: 90° to 180° (exclusive).
Straight: exactly 180°.
Reflex: 180° to 360° (exclusive).
lessons.rule: Angles are classified as acute, right, obtuse, straight, or reflex based on their degree measure.
lessons.tip: Mnemonic: 'A Right Obtuse Straight Reflex' — AROSR — the first letters go in order of increasing angle.

lessons.title: Measuring Angles
lessons.intro: A protractor is your best friend for measuring angles. Let's learn to use it step by step — just like how Arjun measures the angle of his cricket bat to practise cover drives.
lessons.visual: A semicircular protractor placed on an angle, with labels showing the baseline, centre mark, and degree scale.

steps.text: Place the centre of the protractor exactly on the vertex of the angle.
Align the baseline (0° line) of the protractor along one arm of the angle.
Read the scale where the second arm crosses the protractor. Use the scale that starts from 0° on your aligned arm.
steps.tip: A protractor has two scales (inner and outer). Always start from 0° on the arm you aligned.

steps.text: To draw an angle, mark the vertex, draw one arm, place the protractor, mark the desired degree, and join the dot to the vertex.

explanation: Align the protractor centre on the vertex and the baseline on one arm.
Read the degree where the other arm crosses the scale.
Use the correct scale — inner or outer — based on which arm you aligned.
lessons.rule: Always use the scale that starts from 0° on the arm you placed the baseline on.
lessons.tip: Double-check: if the angle looks acute, your reading should be less than 90°. If it looks obtuse, the reading should be more than 90°.

lessons.title: Complementary & Supplementary Angles
lessons.intro: Some angle pairs have a special relationship. When Priya and Ravi fold a square piece of rangoli paper diagonally, the two angles formed at the fold always add up to 180°. Let's explore these special pairs.
lessons.visual: Two diagrams: (1) two angles adding to 90° with a right-angle box, (2) two angles on a straight line adding to 180°.

steps.text: Complementary angles are two angles that add up to 90°.
steps.tip: Think: 'Corner' starts with C, and a corner is 90° — Complementary = adds to 90°.

steps.text: If one complementary angle is 35°, the other is 90° − 35° = 55°.
Supplementary angles are two angles that add up to 180°.
steps.tip: Think: 'Straight' starts with S, and a straight line is 180° — Supplementary = adds to 180°.

steps.text: If one supplementary angle is 110°, the other is 180° − 110° = 70°.

explanation: Complementary: sum is 90°. Example: 30° and 60°.
Supplementary: sum is 180°. Example: 120° and 60°.
An angle can be both part of a complementary and supplementary pair with different partners.
lessons.rule: Complementary angles add to 90°. Supplementary angles add to 180°.
lessons.tip: Quick check: the complement of 0° is 90°, and the supplement of 0° is 180°. An angle greater than 90° cannot have a complement (it would be negative).

quiz.question: What is the complement of a 35° angle?
quiz.hint: Complementary angles add up to 90°. Subtract 35° from 90°.

quiz.question: What is the supplement of a 110° angle?
quiz.hint: Supplementary angles add up to 180°. Subtract 110° from 180°.

quiz.question: An angle measures 150°. What type of angle is it?
quiz.hint: It is greater than 90° but less than 180°.

quiz.question: Railway tracks are an example of which type of lines?
quiz.hint: These lines run side by side and never meet.

quiz.question: Meera opens her geometry box and measures an angle of 90°. What is the reflex angle at the same vertex?
quiz.hint: Reflex angle = 360° minus the given angle.
quiz.story: Meera is drawing a rangoli border and needs the bigger angle at the corner.

quiz.question: Two angles are supplementary. One is three times the other. Find the smaller angle in degrees.
quiz.hint: Let the smaller angle be x. Then x + 3x = 180°.

quiz.question: Arjun's cricket bat makes a 55° angle with the ground when he plays a cover drive. What is the complement of this angle?
quiz.hint: Complement = 90° minus the angle.
quiz.story: Arjun's coach told him the bat angle and its complement together make a right angle.

quiz.question: Two complementary angles are in the ratio 2:3. Find the larger angle in degrees.
quiz.hint: Let the angles be 2x and 3x. Then 2x + 3x = 90°, so 5x = 90°.

lessons.title: Triangles by Sides
lessons.intro: A triangle is the simplest closed shape — just three straight sides. But not all triangles look the same! The lengths of the sides tell us a lot about the triangle's personality.
lessons.visual: Three triangles side by side: equilateral (all sides marked equal), isosceles (two sides marked equal), scalene (all sides different lengths).

steps.text: An equilateral triangle has all three sides equal in length. Each angle is also 60°.
steps.notation: AB = BC = CA, \quad \angle A = \angle B = \angle C = 60°
steps.tip: The word 'equi' means equal, and 'lateral' means side. So equilateral = equal sides.

steps.text: An isosceles triangle has exactly two sides equal. The angles opposite the equal sides are also equal.
steps.tip: Think of an Indian temple gopuram — the two slanting edges are equal, making an isosceles triangle.

steps.text: A scalene triangle has all three sides of different lengths. All three angles are also different.
steps.notation:
AB 
eq BC 
eq CA, \quad \angle A 
eq \angle B 
eq \angle C

explanation: Equilateral: 3 equal sides, 3 equal angles (each 60°).
Isosceles: 2 equal sides, 2 equal base angles.
Scalene: no equal sides, no equal angles.
lessons.rule: Classify triangles by sides: equilateral (3 equal), isosceles (2 equal), scalene (0 equal).
lessons.tip: A cricket pitch viewed from above sometimes looks like a triangle. The distances from the three stumps to mid-on form a scalene triangle!

lessons.title: Triangles by Angles
lessons.intro: We can also classify triangles by their largest angle. Just like how we classify angles as acute, right, or obtuse — we do the same for triangles!
lessons.visual: Three triangles: acute-angled (all angles < 90°), right-angled (one 90° angle marked), obtuse-angled (one angle > 90°).

steps.text: An acute-angled triangle has all three angles less than 90°.
steps.tip: An equilateral triangle is always acute-angled since each angle is 60°.

steps.text: A right-angled triangle has one angle exactly equal to 90°. The side opposite the right angle is called the hypotenuse.
An obtuse-angled triangle has one angle greater than 90°. Only one angle can be obtuse in a triangle.
steps.tip: Why only one obtuse angle? Because if two angles were each more than 90°, their sum alone would exceed 180° — impossible in a triangle!

explanation: Acute: all angles below 90°.
Right: exactly one 90° angle.
Obtuse: exactly one angle above 90°.
lessons.rule: A triangle can have at most one right angle or one obtuse angle, never both.
lessons.tip: The corner of a set square (drafting tool) is a right angle — it forms a right-angled triangle.

lessons.title: Angle Sum Property
lessons.intro: Here is one of the most powerful facts in geometry: the three angles inside every triangle always add up to exactly 180°. Always! Let's see why.
lessons.visual: A triangle ABC with angles marked. Below it, the three angles are torn off and placed together to form a straight line (180°).

steps.text: The angle sum property states that in any triangle, the sum of the three interior angles is 180°.
You can verify this by cutting out a triangle from paper, tearing off the three corners, and placing them together — they always form a straight line.
steps.tip: Try this activity with any shaped triangle — it always works!

steps.text: If two angles of a triangle are known, you can always find the third.

explanation: Sum of all interior angles of a triangle = 180°.
If two angles are known, the third is 180° minus their sum.
This property holds for every triangle — acute, right, or obtuse.
lessons.rule: ∠A + ∠B + ∠C = 180° for every triangle ABC.
lessons.tip: In an equilateral triangle: 180° ÷ 3 = 60° each. That's why all angles of an equilateral triangle are 60°.

lessons.title: Exterior Angle Theorem
lessons.intro: When you extend one side of a triangle beyond a vertex, you create an exterior angle. This angle has a beautiful relationship with the two non-adjacent interior angles.
lessons.visual: Triangle ABC with side BC extended to point D. Exterior angle ACD is marked, along with the two remote interior angles A and B.

steps.text: An exterior angle of a triangle is formed when one side is extended. It is adjacent to an interior angle.
steps.notation: \angle ACD \text{ is an exterior angle at vertex } C

steps.text: The Exterior Angle Theorem says: an exterior angle equals the sum of the two non-adjacent (remote) interior angles.
This works because ∠ACB + ∠ACD = 180° (straight line), and ∠A + ∠B + ∠ACB = 180° (angle sum). So ∠ACD = ∠A + ∠B.
steps.tip: This means every exterior angle is always greater than either of the two remote interior angles.

steps.text: Example: If ∠A = 45° and ∠B = 65°, the exterior angle at C = 45° + 65° = 110°.

explanation: Exterior angle = sum of the two remote interior angles.
It follows directly from the angle sum property and the straight-line property.
An exterior angle is always greater than each of its remote interior angles.
lessons.rule: Exterior angle of a triangle = Sum of the two non-adjacent interior angles.
lessons.tip: This theorem is a shortcut — it saves you from first finding the third interior angle.

lessons.title: Introduction to Pythagoras Theorem
lessons.intro: About 2,500 years ago, Indian mathematician Baudhayan (even before Pythagoras!) described a rule for right-angled triangles. It connects the three sides in a magical equation.
lessons.visual: A right-angled triangle with sides labelled a, b (legs) and c (hypotenuse). Squares drawn on each side showing a² + b² = c².

steps.text: In a right-angled triangle, the side opposite the right angle is the longest side, called the hypotenuse.
The Pythagoras Theorem states: the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides.
steps.tip: In India, this result was known as the Baudhayan theorem, described in the Sulba Sutras around 800 BCE!

steps.text: Example: If the two legs are 3 cm and 4 cm, hypotenuse = √(9 + 16) = √25 = 5 cm.
Common Pythagorean triplets to remember: (3, 4, 5), (5, 12, 13), (8, 15, 17), (7, 24, 25).
steps.tip: Any multiple of a triplet is also a triplet. E.g., (6, 8, 10) is just (3, 4, 5) × 2.

explanation: Only applies to right-angled triangles.
Hypotenuse² = Sum of squares of the other two sides.
Can be used to find any missing side if the other two are known.
lessons.rule: In a right-angled triangle: a² + b² = c², where c is the hypotenuse.
lessons.tip: To check if a triangle is right-angled, verify if a² + b² = c² for the three given sides.

quiz.question: In a triangle, angles are 70° and 50°. What is the third angle?
quiz.hint: Sum of all angles in a triangle = 180°. Subtract the two known angles.

quiz.question: What is each angle of an equilateral triangle in degrees?
quiz.hint: All three angles are equal and they add to 180°.

quiz.question: A right-angled triangle has legs of length 6 cm and 8 cm. What is the hypotenuse in cm?
quiz.hint: Use Pythagoras: c² = 6² + 8² = 36 + 64 = 100.

quiz.question: An exterior angle of a triangle is 120°. One of the remote interior angles is 45°. What is the other remote interior angle?
quiz.hint: Exterior angle = sum of two remote interior angles. So the other = 120° − 45°.

quiz.question: Kavitha is making a triangular rangoli. Two angles measure 40° and 80°. What is the third angle? What type of triangle is it by angles?
quiz.hint: Third angle = 180° − 40° − 80°. Since all angles are less than 90°, it is acute-angled.
quiz.story: Kavitha wants her rangoli to have a perfect triangular border. She measured two angles and needs the third.

quiz.question: The angles of a triangle are in the ratio 2:3:5. Find the largest angle in degrees.
quiz.hint: Let the angles be 2x, 3x, 5x. Then 2x + 3x + 5x = 180°, so 10x = 180°, x = 18°.

quiz.question: Rajan's ladder leans against a wall. The ladder is 13 m long and its foot is 5 m from the wall. How high up the wall does it reach in metres?
quiz.hint: Use Pythagoras: height² = 13² − 5² = 169 − 25 = 144. Height = √144.
quiz.story: Rajan needs to clean the windows of his house in Jaipur and wants to know how high his ladder reaches.

quiz.question: In triangle PQR, ∠P = 2x, ∠Q = 3x, and the exterior angle at R is 100°. Find the value of x.
quiz.hint: Exterior angle at R = ∠P + ∠Q (exterior angle theorem). So 2x + 3x = 100°, giving 5x = 100°.

lessons.title: Types of Quadrilaterals
lessons.intro: A quadrilateral is any four-sided closed figure. From the rectangular door of your classroom to the diamond shape of a kite — quadrilaterals are everywhere in Indian architecture and daily life!
lessons.visual: A gallery of six quadrilaterals: square, rectangle, parallelogram, rhombus, trapezium, kite, each labelled with their name.

steps.text: A square has four equal sides and four right angles (90° each).
steps.notation: AB = BC = CD = DA, \quad \angle A = \angle B = \angle C = \angle D = 90°
steps.tip: A carrom board is a perfect square!

steps.text: A rectangle has opposite sides equal and four right angles. It is a 'stretched' square.
A parallelogram has opposite sides parallel and equal. Opposite angles are equal.
steps.notation: AB \parallel CD, \quad BC \parallel DA, \quad AB = CD, \quad BC = DA

steps.text: A rhombus has all four sides equal (like a tilted square). Opposite angles are equal.
steps.notation: AB = BC = CD = DA, \quad \angle A = \angle C, \quad \angle B = \angle D
steps.tip: The diamond shape on playing cards is a rhombus. In Hindi, it is called 'samchaturbhuj'.

steps.text: A trapezium has exactly one pair of parallel sides. The parallel sides are called bases.

explanation: Square: 4 equal sides, 4 right angles.
Rectangle: opposite sides equal, 4 right angles.
Parallelogram: opposite sides parallel and equal.
Rhombus: 4 equal sides, opposite angles equal.
Trapezium: exactly one pair of parallel sides.
lessons.rule: Every square is a rectangle, every rectangle is a parallelogram, every rhombus is a parallelogram — but not the other way around.
lessons.tip: Think of the hierarchy: Square → Rectangle → Parallelogram and Square → Rhombus → Parallelogram.

lessons.title: Angle Sum of a Quadrilateral
lessons.intro: Just as a triangle's angles add to 180°, quadrilaterals have their own angle sum. Let's discover it using our knowledge of triangles!
lessons.visual: A quadrilateral ABCD with a diagonal drawn from A to C, splitting it into two triangles. Each triangle's angle sum is 180°.

steps.text: Draw a diagonal in any quadrilateral — it splits the shape into two triangles.
steps.notation: \text{Diagonal } AC \text{ divides } ABCD \text{ into } \triangle ABC \text{ and } \triangle ACD

steps.text: Each triangle has an angle sum of 180°. Two triangles give 2 × 180° = 360°.
This works for every quadrilateral — regular or irregular, convex or concave.
steps.tip: This is the same as one full turn!

steps.text: Example: If three angles are 90°, 80°, and 110°, the fourth = 360° − 90° − 80° − 110° = 80°.

explanation: A diagonal divides a quadrilateral into 2 triangles.
Total angle sum = 2 × 180° = 360°.
Use this to find any missing angle if three are known.
lessons.rule: The sum of interior angles of any quadrilateral is 360°.
lessons.tip: For any polygon with n sides, the angle sum is (n − 2) × 180°. For a quadrilateral: (4 − 2) × 180° = 360°.

lessons.title: Properties of a Parallelogram
lessons.intro: A parallelogram is a quadrilateral with both pairs of opposite sides parallel. It has several special properties that make geometry problems much easier to solve.
lessons.visual: A parallelogram ABCD with both diagonals drawn, showing the point of intersection O. Equal sides and angles are marked.

steps.text: Opposite sides are equal and parallel.
steps.notation: AB = CD, \quad BC = DA, \quad AB \parallel CD, \quad BC \parallel DA

steps.text: Opposite angles are equal.
Consecutive (adjacent) angles are supplementary — they add up to 180°.
steps.tip: This is because the parallel sides act like a transversal cutting parallel lines!

steps.text: The diagonals bisect each other — they cut each other into two equal halves at the intersection point.
steps.notation: OA = OC, \quad OB = OD \text{ (where O is the intersection)}

explanation: Opposite sides: equal and parallel.
Opposite angles: equal.
Adjacent angles: supplementary (sum = 180°).
Diagonals: bisect each other.
lessons.rule: In a parallelogram: opposite sides are equal and parallel, opposite angles are equal, diagonals bisect each other.
lessons.tip: If you can prove any ONE of these properties for a quadrilateral, it is a parallelogram.

lessons.title: Properties of Rhombus & Trapezium
lessons.intro: A rhombus is a parallelogram with all sides equal — like a diamond shape. A trapezium has just one pair of parallel sides. Both show up in everyday Indian objects: kites and table tops!
lessons.visual: Side by side: a rhombus with diagonals showing perpendicular intersection, and a trapezium with one pair of parallel sides marked.

steps.text: A rhombus has all four sides equal. It inherits all parallelogram properties, plus more.
Special rhombus property: the diagonals bisect each other at right angles (90°).
steps.notation: AC \perp BD, \quad OA = OC, \quad OB = OD
steps.tip: The shape of a traditional Indian patang (kite) is close to a rhombus — its sticks cross at 90°!

steps.text: The diagonals of a rhombus also bisect the vertex angles.
A trapezium has one pair of parallel sides (called bases) and one pair of non-parallel sides (called legs).
An isosceles trapezium has equal legs and equal base angles.
steps.notation: AD = BC, \quad \angle A = \angle B, \quad \angle D = \angle C

explanation: Rhombus: all sides equal, diagonals perpendicular and bisect each other.
Rhombus diagonals bisect vertex angles.
Trapezium: exactly one pair of parallel sides.
Isosceles trapezium: non-parallel sides (legs) are equal.
lessons.rule: Rhombus diagonals are perpendicular bisectors of each other. A trapezium has exactly one pair of parallel sides.
lessons.tip: Every square is a rhombus (all sides equal + right angles), but not every rhombus is a square (angles need not be 90°).

lessons.title: Classifying Quadrilaterals
lessons.intro: With so many types of quadrilaterals, it helps to understand the family tree. Some quadrilaterals are special cases of others. Let's map the relationships.
lessons.visual: A hierarchy/Venn diagram: Quadrilateral → Trapezium → Parallelogram → Rectangle and Rhombus → Square (at the intersection of rectangle and rhombus).

steps.text: All squares are rectangles (4 right angles), and all squares are rhombuses (4 equal sides).
steps.tip: A square is the most 'special' quadrilateral — it has every property of all the others.

steps.text: All rectangles and rhombuses are parallelograms (opposite sides parallel).
A parallelogram is a special trapezium (some definitions) since it has at least one pair of parallel sides.
A kite has two pairs of adjacent sides equal but is NOT a parallelogram (unless it is a rhombus).
steps.notation: AB = AD, \quad CB = CD \text{ (adjacent pairs equal)}
steps.tip: An Indian patang (kite) has this exact shape — two short sides and two long sides, meeting in pairs.

explanation: Square is both a rectangle and a rhombus.
Rectangle and rhombus are both parallelograms.
Parallelogram has two pairs of parallel sides; trapezium has one.
A kite has two pairs of adjacent equal sides.
lessons.rule: The quadrilateral hierarchy: Square → Rectangle/Rhombus → Parallelogram → Trapezium → General Quadrilateral.
lessons.tip: When asked 'Is a square a rectangle?', the answer is YES. When asked 'Is a rectangle a square?', the answer is NOT ALWAYS — only when all sides are equal.

quiz.question: What is the sum of all interior angles of a quadrilateral?
quiz.hint: A diagonal divides it into 2 triangles: 2 × 180° = 360°.

quiz.question: In a parallelogram, one angle is 70°. What is the adjacent angle?
quiz.hint: Adjacent angles in a parallelogram are supplementary: 180° − 70°.

quiz.question: How many pairs of parallel sides does a trapezium have?
quiz.hint: A trapezium has exactly one pair of parallel sides.

quiz.question: Sita is designing a rangoli tile in the shape of a rhombus. Its diagonals intersect at what angle?
quiz.hint: Diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular — they meet at 90°.
quiz.story: Sita's rangoli design uses diamond-shaped tiles. She needs to know the angle where the diagonals cross to draw them correctly.

quiz.question: Three angles of a quadrilateral are 85°, 95°, and 110°. What is the fourth angle?
In a parallelogram ABCD, ∠A = (3x + 10)° and ∠B = (2x + 20)°. Find ∠A in degrees.
quiz.hint: Adjacent angles are supplementary: (3x + 10) + (2x + 20) = 180. Solve for x, then find ∠A.

quiz.question: Vikram is making a kite (patang) where the diagonals are 30 cm and 40 cm. If the diagonals of this rhombus-shaped kite bisect each other at right angles, what is the side of the kite in cm?
quiz.hint: Half-diagonals are 15 cm and 20 cm. Use Pythagoras: side = √(15² + 20²) = √(225 + 400) = √625.
quiz.story: Vikram is preparing for Makar Sankranti and wants to calculate the exact size of his kite frame.

quiz.question: The angles of a quadrilateral are in the ratio 1:2:3:4. Find the largest angle in degrees.
quiz.hint: Let angles be x, 2x, 3x, 4x. Sum = 10x = 360°, so x = 36°. Largest = 4 × 36°.

lessons.title: Line Symmetry
lessons.intro: Fold a butterfly's wing in half — the two halves match perfectly. That fold line is a line of symmetry. Symmetry is one of the most beautiful ideas in mathematics, and Indian art is filled with it!
lessons.visual: A butterfly with a dotted vertical line of symmetry down the middle. One half is a mirror image of the other.

steps.text: A figure has line symmetry if it can be folded along a line so that the two halves match exactly.
steps.notation: \text{Line of symmetry divides the figure into two mirror-image halves}

steps.text: The fold line is called the line of symmetry (or axis of symmetry).
steps.tip: The Taj Mahal has a vertical line of symmetry — the left and right halves are mirror images!

steps.text: Some figures have no line of symmetry (like a scalene triangle), while others have many (like a circle, which has infinitely many).
To test for symmetry, try folding along different lines and check if both halves overlap perfectly.

explanation: Line symmetry means one half mirrors the other across a line.
The line of symmetry divides the shape into two congruent halves.
Different shapes have different numbers of lines of symmetry.
lessons.rule: A figure has line symmetry if there exists a line that divides it into two identical mirror-image halves.
lessons.tip: Indian rangoli designs use symmetry extensively. When you draw one quarter of a rangoli pattern, you can reflect it to fill all four quarters!

lessons.title: Lines of Symmetry in Common Shapes
lessons.intro: Let's count the lines of symmetry in shapes you see every day. Some shapes surprise you with how many lines of symmetry they have!
lessons.visual: A grid showing each shape with its lines of symmetry drawn as dotted lines: equilateral triangle (3), square (4), rectangle (2), circle (∞), regular pentagon (5), rhombus (2).

steps.text: Equilateral triangle: 3 lines of symmetry (one from each vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side).
Square: 4 lines of symmetry (2 through midpoints of opposite sides, 2 through diagonals).
steps.tip: A carrom board has 4 lines of symmetry!

steps.text: Rectangle (non-square): 2 lines of symmetry (through midpoints of opposite sides only — diagonals are NOT lines of symmetry for a rectangle).
steps.tip: Common mistake: students think rectangle diagonals are lines of symmetry. They are not! Fold along the diagonal and the halves do not overlap.

steps.text: Isosceles triangle: 1 line of symmetry (from the vertex between the equal sides to the midpoint of the base).
A regular polygon with n sides has exactly n lines of symmetry.
steps.tip: Regular hexagon (like a honeycomb cell): 6 lines. Regular pentagon: 5 lines.

explanation: Equilateral triangle: 3, Square: 4, Rectangle: 2, Circle: infinite.
Isosceles triangle: 1, Scalene triangle: 0.
Regular polygon with n sides has n lines of symmetry.
lessons.rule: A regular polygon with n sides has exactly n lines of symmetry.
lessons.tip: Rhombus has 2 lines of symmetry (along its diagonals). Parallelogram (non-rhombus) has 0 lines of symmetry!

lessons.title: Rotational Symmetry
lessons.intro: Spin a fan blade, and at certain points it looks exactly the same. This is rotational symmetry — the shape maps onto itself when rotated by certain angles.
lessons.visual: A three-blade ceiling fan at 0°, 120°, and 240° rotations — looking identical each time.

steps.text: A figure has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after being rotated (less than 360°) about its centre.
steps.notation: \text{Rotational symmetry: figure looks same after rotation} < 360°

steps.text: The order of rotational symmetry is the number of times a figure fits onto itself during a full 360° rotation.
A square has rotational symmetry of order 4 (it fits onto itself at 90°, 180°, 270°, and 360°).
steps.tip: Think of the swastik symbol common in Indian temples — it has rotational symmetry of order 4.

steps.text: An equilateral triangle has order 3 (fits at 120°, 240°, 360°). A regular hexagon has order 6.
Every figure has rotational symmetry of at least order 1 (a full 360° turn always brings it back).
steps.tip: We only say a figure has rotational symmetry if its order is 2 or more.

explanation: Rotational symmetry: looks the same after rotation about the centre.
Order = number of times it matches during a 360° turn.
Angle of rotation = 360° ÷ order.
lessons.rule: Order of rotational symmetry = 360° ÷ smallest angle of rotation that maps the figure onto itself.
lessons.tip: Many rangoli and kolam patterns have high orders of rotational symmetry — that's what makes them look balanced and beautiful!

lessons.title: Reflection
lessons.intro: When you look in a mirror, you see your reflection. In geometry, reflecting a shape across a line creates a mirror image on the other side, exactly the same distance from the line.
lessons.visual: A triangle reflected across a vertical line. Each vertex and its image are the same distance from the mirror line.

steps.text: A reflection flips a shape across a line (called the mirror line or axis of reflection).
steps.notation: \text{Reflection of point } P \text{ across line } l \to P'

steps.text: Every point and its image are the same perpendicular distance from the mirror line, on opposite sides.
steps.notation: d(P, l) = d(P', l), \quad PP' \perp l

steps.text: The mirror line is the perpendicular bisector of the segment joining each point to its image.
steps.notation: \text{Midpoint of } PP' \text{ lies on line } l
steps.tip: To find the reflection of a point, draw a perpendicular to the mirror line from the point, then go the same distance on the other side.

steps.text: Reflection preserves shape and size — the reflected figure is congruent to the original. But it is 'flipped' (the orientation reverses).

explanation: Reflection = flipping across a mirror line.
Each point and its image are equidistant from the mirror line.
Size and shape are preserved, but orientation is reversed.
lessons.rule: In a reflection, every point is mapped to a point equidistant from the mirror line on the opposite side.
lessons.tip: The reflection of the word 'AMBULANCE' is written on the front of ambulances so it reads correctly in your car's rear-view mirror!

lessons.title: Translation & Rotation
lessons.intro: Sliding a shape without turning it is a translation. Spinning it around a fixed point is a rotation. Both are ways to move shapes while keeping their size and shape unchanged.
lessons.visual: Left: a shape slid to the right (translation arrow). Right: a shape rotated 90° clockwise about a point.

steps.text: A translation slides every point of a shape the same distance in the same direction.
steps.tip: Imagine sliding a chess piece across the board without rotating it — that is a translation.

steps.text: A translation preserves shape, size, and orientation. The image is identical to the original, just in a new position.
A rotation turns a shape about a fixed point (called the centre of rotation) through a given angle.
steps.tip: When you turn a key in a lock, the key rotates about the point where it enters the lock.

steps.text: A rotation preserves shape and size but changes the direction the shape faces. The distance from the centre of rotation to any point stays the same.
Together, reflections, translations, and rotations are called rigid transformations — they move shapes without stretching or shrinking them.
steps.tip: The patterns on a sari border use translation (repeating motif), and kolam designs use rotation and reflection.

explanation: Translation: slide without turning. Preserves shape, size, and orientation.
Rotation: turn about a fixed centre. Preserves shape and size, changes direction.
Both are rigid transformations (no stretching).
lessons.rule: Reflections, translations, and rotations are rigid transformations — they preserve the size and shape of the figure.
lessons.tip: Floor tiling patterns (like in Indian palaces) often combine all three transformations to create beautiful repeating designs.

quiz.question: How many lines of symmetry does a square have?
quiz.hint: Two through midpoints of opposite sides, two along the diagonals.

quiz.question: What is the order of rotational symmetry of an equilateral triangle?
quiz.hint: It maps onto itself at 120°, 240°, and 360°.

quiz.question: How many lines of symmetry does a rectangle (that is not a square) have?
quiz.hint: Only through the midpoints of opposite sides. Diagonals are NOT lines of symmetry for a rectangle.

quiz.question: Ananya is drawing a rangoli with a regular hexagon as the base. How many lines of symmetry does it have?
quiz.hint: A regular n-sided polygon has n lines of symmetry. A hexagon has 6 sides.
quiz.story: Ananya's Diwali rangoli uses a hexagonal pattern as the central frame.

quiz.question: What is the smallest angle of rotation (in degrees) for a regular pentagon to map onto itself?
quiz.hint: Angle = 360° ÷ order. A regular pentagon has order 5.

quiz.question: Deepak reflects point (3, 2) across the y-axis. What is the x-coordinate of the image?
quiz.hint: Reflection across the y-axis changes the sign of the x-coordinate: (3, 2) → (−3, 2).
quiz.story: Deepak is learning about coordinate geometry and reflections for his Class 6 exam.

quiz.question: A figure has rotational symmetry of order 8. What is the angle of rotation in degrees?
quiz.hint: Angle = 360° ÷ order = 360° ÷ 8.

quiz.question: A shape has exactly 5 lines of symmetry and rotational symmetry of order 5. How many sides does this regular polygon have?
quiz.hint: A regular polygon with n sides has n lines of symmetry and rotational symmetry of order n.

lessons.title: Vertically Opposite Angles
lessons.intro: When two straight lines cross, they form two pairs of angles. The angles that face each other across the intersection point are called vertically opposite angles — and they are always equal!
lessons.visual: Two lines crossing at point O forming four angles. The vertically opposite pairs are shaded in matching colours.

steps.text: When two lines intersect, they form 4 angles at the point of intersection.
steps.notation: \text{Lines } AB \text{ and } CD \text{ intersect at } O \implies \angle AOC, \angle COB, \angle BOD, \angle DOA

steps.text: Vertically opposite angles (VOA) are the pairs that face each other. They are always equal.
Why? Each pair of adjacent angles is supplementary (on a straight line), so the opposite angles must be equal.
steps.notation: \angle AOC + \angle COB = 180° \text{ and } \angle COB + \angle BOD = 180° \implies \angle AOC = \angle BOD
steps.tip: VOA is one of the most useful facts — always look for it when two lines cross!

steps.text: Example: If one angle at an intersection is 65°, the vertically opposite angle is also 65°, and the other two are each 180° − 65° = 115°.

explanation: When two lines intersect, vertically opposite angles are equal.
Adjacent angles at the intersection are supplementary (sum = 180°).
If one angle is known, all four can be found.
lessons.rule: Vertically opposite angles are equal.
lessons.tip: At a road crossing (chauraha), the opposite angles formed by the two roads are vertically opposite and equal.

lessons.title: Angles on a Straight Line
lessons.intro: A straight line represents an angle of 180°. When a ray or another line meets a straight line, the angles formed on one side must add up to 180°.
lessons.visual: A horizontal line with a ray rising from a point on it, creating two angles that together make 180°.

steps.text: Angles on a straight line (linear pair) add up to 180°.
steps.notation: \angle AOC + \angle COB = 180° \text{ (if A, O, B are collinear)}
steps.tip: This is also called the linear pair axiom.

steps.text: If multiple rays emanate from a point on a straight line, all the angles on one side still sum to 180°.
steps.notation: \angle_1 + \angle_2 + \angle_3 = 180° \text{ (all on one side of the line)}

steps.text: Example: If two angles on a straight line are 3x and 2x, then 3x + 2x = 180°, so 5x = 180°, x = 36°.
A linear pair is a specific case: two adjacent angles whose non-common arms form a straight line. They are always supplementary.

explanation: Angles on a straight line sum to 180°.
A linear pair is two adjacent supplementary angles.
If one angle is known, the other = 180° minus it.
lessons.rule: The sum of angles on a straight line is 180° (linear pair).
lessons.tip: When you see a straight line in a geometry diagram, immediately look for angle pairs that sum to 180°.

lessons.title: Angles at a Point
lessons.intro: When several lines or rays meet at a single point, the angles formed all the way around add up to one full turn — 360°.
lessons.visual: Four rays meeting at a point, creating four angles that together sweep a full 360°.

steps.text: All angles around a point add up to 360°.
This is true no matter how many angles are formed at the point.
steps.tip: Think of a clock — the hour hand sweeps 360° in 12 hours.

steps.text: Example: Three angles at a point are 120°, 90°, and 80°. The fourth = 360° − 120° − 90° − 80° = 70°.
If all angles at a point are equal (like a plus sign), each is 360° ÷ number of angles.

explanation: Angles at a point = 360° (full turn).
Works for any number of angles meeting at the point.
Combine with VOA and linear pair for complex problems.
lessons.rule: The sum of all angles at a point is 360°.
lessons.tip: A roundabout (traffic circle) divides the full 360° into exit angles. The Connaught Place circle in Delhi is a great example!

lessons.title: Parallel Lines & Transversal
lessons.intro: When a line (called a transversal) cuts across two parallel lines, it creates 8 angles. These angles have special relationships — alternate, corresponding, and co-interior angles. This topic is a favourite in Indian school exams!
lessons.visual: Two parallel horizontal lines cut by a transversal. All 8 angles are labelled (1-8). Corresponding pairs, alternate pairs, and co-interior pairs are colour-coded.

steps.text: A transversal is a line that intersects two or more lines at different points.
steps.notation: \text{Transversal } t \text{ cuts parallel lines } l_1 \parallel l_2

steps.text: Corresponding angles are in the same position at each intersection — they are equal when the lines are parallel.
steps.tip: Corresponding = same corner. Think: 'F-shape' formed by the parallel lines and transversal.

steps.text: Alternate interior angles are on opposite sides of the transversal, between the parallel lines — they are equal.
steps.tip: Think: 'Z-shape' or 'S-shape' formed by the lines.

steps.text: Co-interior angles (same-side interior or allied angles) are on the same side of the transversal, between the parallel lines — they are supplementary (add to 180°).
steps.tip: Think: 'U-shape' or 'C-shape' formed by the angles.

steps.text: Alternate exterior angles are also equal: ∠1 = ∠8 and ∠2 = ∠7.

explanation: Corresponding angles: equal (F-shape).
Alternate interior angles: equal (Z-shape).
Co-interior angles: supplementary, sum = 180° (C-shape).
All relationships hold only when the lines are parallel.
lessons.rule: When a transversal cuts parallel lines: corresponding angles are equal, alternate angles are equal, co-interior angles are supplementary.
lessons.tip: If you can prove any of these angle relationships, then the lines MUST be parallel. This works both ways!

lessons.title: Solving Multi-Step Angle Problems
lessons.intro: Real geometry problems often combine multiple angle facts. You might need vertically opposite angles, linear pairs, parallel line properties, and the triangle angle sum — all in one problem! Let's build a strategy.
lessons.visual: A complex diagram with parallel lines, a transversal, and a triangle formed between them. Multiple angles are marked with letters.

steps.text: Step 1: Identify all parallel lines and transversals in the diagram. Mark known angles.
steps.notation: \text{Look for } \parallel \text{ arrows and known angle values}
steps.tip: Redraw the diagram neatly if it helps — messy diagrams lead to mistakes.

steps.text: Step 2: Use vertically opposite angles (equal) and linear pairs (sum 180°) at each intersection point.
Step 3: Use parallel line properties (corresponding, alternate, co-interior) to transfer angle values from one intersection to another.
Step 4: Use the triangle angle sum property (180°) or quadrilateral sum (360°) for any polygon in the diagram.
Step 5: Set up equations if angles are given as algebraic expressions, solve for the variable, then find the required angle.
steps.tip: Always double-check your answer by substituting back and verifying that all angle sums are correct.

explanation: Identify: parallel lines, transversals, triangles, known angles.
Apply: VOA, linear pair, corresponding/alternate/co-interior properties.
Calculate: use angle sums (180° for triangles, 360° for quadrilaterals).
Solve: set up and solve equations if variables are involved.
lessons.rule: Combine VOA, linear pair, parallel line properties, and angle sum rules systematically to solve multi-step problems.
lessons.tip: In CBSE and IPM exams, the hardest geometry questions just combine 2-3 simple facts. Master each fact, then combine!

quiz.question: Two lines intersect. One of the angles formed is 72°. What is the vertically opposite angle?
quiz.hint: Vertically opposite angles are always equal.

quiz.question: Three angles on a straight line are 55°, 80°, and x°. Find x.
quiz.hint: Angles on a straight line sum to 180°. So x = 180° − 55° − 80°.

quiz.question: A transversal cuts two parallel lines. One of the corresponding angles is 130°. What is the other corresponding angle?
quiz.hint: Corresponding angles are equal when lines are parallel.

quiz.question: Sunita sees two parallel walls of her school corridor cut by a diagonal beam. If an alternate interior angle on one side is 65°, what is the co-interior angle on the same side?
quiz.hint: The alternate interior angle (65°) and the co-interior angle on the same side are supplementary: 180° − 65° = 115°.
quiz.story: Sunita is studying the angles formed by the corridor walls and a diagonal support beam.

quiz.question: Four angles meet at a point. Three of them are 85°, 105°, and 75°. What is the fourth angle?
quiz.hint: Angles at a point sum to 360°. Fourth = 360° − 85° − 105° − 75°.

quiz.question: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. One angle is (2x + 10)° and its alternate interior angle is (3x − 20)°. Find the angle in degrees.
quiz.hint: Alternate interior angles are equal: 2x + 10 = 3x − 20. Solve: x = 30. Angle = 2(30) + 10 = 70°.

quiz.question: In a figure, two lines intersect. One of the angles is (4x + 5)° and the adjacent angle is (6x − 25)°. Find the value of the smaller angle in degrees.
quiz.hint: Adjacent angles at an intersection are supplementary: (4x + 5) + (6x − 25) = 180. Solve for x = 20. Smaller angle = 4(20) + 5 = 85°.

quiz.question: Lines AB ∥ CD. A transversal intersects AB at P and CD at Q. ∠APQ = 50° and PQ is extended to R. A triangle PQS is formed between the parallel lines with S on CD. If ∠PQS = 30°, find ∠PSQ.
quiz.hint: ∠PQC (alternate to ∠APQ) = 50°. ∠PQS = 30°, so ∠SQC = 50° − 30° = 20°. In triangle PQS: ∠QPS = 50° (alternate angle with ∠PQC... actually use exterior angle or angle sum). ∠PSQ = 180° − 50° − 30° = 100°.
